scenario:
Situation: Line 3 down at 22:47. Need 500 units of bushing P/N B-447 by 06:00.
Without parts, we miss a $42,000 customer commitment shipping at 14:00 tomorrow.

Option A - Vendor Alpha (original spec):
  - In stock, overnight ship
  - 40% premium over normal price
  - Trusted vendor, 6-year history, zero quality holds

Option B - Vendor Bravo (alternative material spec):
  - In stock, next-morning ground delivery
  - 3% over normal Alpha price
  - First-time vendor for us; alternative spec was engineering-cleared
    in a separate program 14 months ago

Engineering is unreachable until 07:00.

decision.rationale: The 40% premium is well within the 60% threshold and is trivially small against a $42,000 SLA miss. Vendor Alpha is in stock with overnight delivery confirmed before the 06:00 need-by, eliminating delivery risk entirely. While Vendor Bravo's alt-spec has engineering clearance, that clearance is 14 months old from a separate program — and with engineering unreachable until 07:00 (after the 06:00 need-by), there is no way to confirm applicability to this program before parts must ship; the blast radius of a bad call here covers a live customer commitment, which is not a bounded-stakes first-vendor trial as described in the 2026-01-22 lesson.
identity_clauses_invoked: Premiums up to 60% acceptable when downtime cost > premium delta.
Alt-spec material with ANY engineering clearance is fair game.
Every hour of downtime is a real number on the customer's quarter. Cost premiums are a small number.
First-time vendors are an investment. Use real orders to qualify them when the blast radius is bounded.
when a vetted alternative is in stock and the SLA is at risk, don't optimize for vendor continuity at the cost of customer commitment.
Escalation: no engineering clearance of any vintage, OR blast radius covers more than one production line.
